title: The Return of Joy and Why It Feels Suspicious
researcherTopic: sensorimotor psychotherapy, the body in trauma treatment
metaDescription: For trauma survivors, joy can feel suspicious. Learn why your nervous system flags happiness as a threat and how to welcome positive feelings back.
imageDescription: A single, resilient flower pushes through a crack in ancient stone, its delicate petals unfurling in the warm, golden light of dawn. The scene evokes a sense of gentle, hard-won beauty and the quiet triumph of life returning.
htmlContent:
What happens when the very feeling you’ve been striving for—joy, ease, a moment of genuine peace—finally arrives, only to be met with a profound sense of suspicion? This isn’t a cognitive error or a sign of ingratitude; it is the deeply conditioned response of a nervous system that learned, long ago, that moments of reprieve were often the prelude to danger, the quiet before the storm. For those of us who have navigated the landscape of complex trauma, the return of positive feeling states can feel more threatening than the familiar territory of anxiety or hypervigilance, a neurological bait-and-switch that the body refuses to fall for a second time. The architecture of survival is not built for pleasure, but for prediction, and it will flag anything that deviates from the baseline of remembered threat as a potential error in the system.

The Neuroception of Suspicious Joy

Our experience of the world is not a direct, one-to-one translation of reality; it is a constructed narrative, filtered through the predictive lens of the nervous system. This process, which the researcher Stephen Porges termed neuroception, is an unconscious, sub-cortical scanning of the environment for cues of safety and danger. It happens outside of thought, beneath the level of conscious awareness. For a nervous system shaped by complex trauma, the database of "safe" is infinitesimally small, while the database of "dangerous" is vast and exquisitely detailed. When a moment of joy appears, it doesn’t match the existing data. The system, in its relentless effort to keep us alive, flags it as an anomaly. Is this real? Is it a trick? How long will it last, and what will be the price for this moment of peace? In my years of working in this territory, I’ve seen countless individuals describe this phenomenon as a feeling of waiting for the other shoe to drop, a deep-seated inability to trust goodness.

The mind is not the enemy. The identification with it is.

This isn’t a personal failing. It is the logical, predictable outcome of a system that has been exquisitely trained to anticipate threat. The suspicion is not a thought you are choosing to have; it is a somatic sensation, a visceral tightening, a bracing against an impact that has already happened countless times in the past. We are not reacting to the present moment of joy, but to the ghost of a thousand moments when safety was abruptly withdrawn. The work, then, is not to convince the mind that it is safe, but to offer the body new experiences that can begin to update its deeply held, implicit memories. It’s a process of titration, of introducing small, manageable moments of ease and allowing the system to metabolize them without becoming overwhelmed. We are, in essence, building a new database, one in which joy is not a harbinger of doom but a signal of genuine security. We might find ourselves exploring this territory through practices that anchor us in the present, like those found in our exploration of somatic experiencing.

The Body's Reluctance to Surrender

To truly allow joy to land in the body requires a profound act of surrender—a letting go of the hypervigilant control that has been our primary survival strategy. For many, this feels like a dereliction of duty. The part of us that has been standing guard for decades, scanning the horizon for threats, does not simply clock out because the war is over. In fact, the cessation of active threat can be the most destabilizing time of all. The body, so long organized around a central axis of danger, can feel lost, untethered, and purposeless without it. A client once described this as feeling like a soldier who has returned from a war zone only to find that he cannot tolerate the silence of a suburban street. The absence of noise is, to his nervous system, more terrifying than the sounds of battle he knew how to navigate.

This reluctance is not a sign of being broken; it is a testament to the body's incredible capacity to adapt. The challenge is that the adaptation has outlived its usefulness. The armor that protected us in the past now prevents the warmth of connection and joy from getting in. Pat Ogden, a pioneer in the field of sensorimotor psychotherapy, speaks to the importance of working directly with these physical patterns of holding and defense. We cannot simply tell the body to relax; we must engage it in a conversation, exploring the postures, gestures, and tensions that hold the story of the trauma. It is a slow, respectful process of inquiry, of noticing the impulse to brace when a compliment is offered, or the subtle clenching in the jaw when a moment of laughter arises. It is in these micromoments that the real work of healing takes place, not in grand epiphanies, but in the quiet, consistent act of meeting the body’s fear with compassionate awareness. This process often involves understanding the different trauma responses that have become ingrained in our being.

The Economy of Scarcity and the Return of Plenty

A system conditioned by trauma operates on an economy of scarcity. Love, safety, attunement, and joy were all in short supply, and one learned to subsist on the barest of rations. The idea of an abundance of these resources is not just foreign; it is deeply suspicious. When joy begins to flow more consistently, it can trigger a primal fear of there not being enough, or that its presence now means it will be absent later. This is the zero-sum game of the traumatized psyche. We may find ourselves subtly sabotaging moments of happiness, picking a fight with a loved one, or becoming consumed with anxiety about the future, all in an unconscious effort to return to the familiar baseline of scarcity. It is a strange and painful paradox: the very thing we most desire is the thing we are most conditioned to reject.

Deb Dana's exercise book provides practical nervous system regulation tools Polyvagal Exercises for Safety and Connection (paid link).

The wellness industry sells solutions to problems it helps you believe you have.

Breaking free from this economy requires a radical re-imagining of what is possible. It involves the conscious recognition that joy is not a finite resource that gets used up. It is a wellspring, an inherent capacity of the human spirit that has been obscured, but not extinguished. The work is to challenge the belief that we must "earn" our moments of peace, or that they must be paid for with subsequent pain. This is a deeply ingrained cultural and familial narrative for many, the idea that one must suffer for their joy. We can begin to dismantle this by intentionally savoring small moments of pleasure—the warmth of the sun on the skin, the taste of a perfectly ripe piece of fruit, the sound of a beloved piece of music. These are not trivial exercises; they are potent acts of rebellion against the tyranny of scarcity. They are data points for the nervous system, proving that goodness can exist without a price tag. It is a slow and steady accumulation of evidence that we are, in fact, worthy of abundance, a concept that is often at odds with the narratives we have constructed about ourselves, as we have discussed in the context of healing shame.

When Joy Feels Like a Betrayal

For some, the arrival of joy can be complicated by a sense of guilt or betrayal. To feel happy can feel like a betrayal of the parts of us that are still in pain, or a betrayal of the past self who suffered so much. It can feel as though we are forgetting, moving on, or invalidating the reality of what happened. This is particularly true when the trauma is relational, when it involves the loss of a significant relationship or a profound sense of abandonment. How can I be happy when this other person is still suffering, or when the child I once was never had the chance to be? This is a tender and important territory to navigate, and it requires a great deal of compassion for all parts of our experience.

The reframe here is to understand that genuine joy is not an erasure of the past, but an integration of it. It is the fruit of the long, hard work of healing. It does not invalidate the suffering; it honors it by demonstrating that it was not in vain. Our capacity to feel joy is not a sign that we have forgotten, but a sign that we have survived. It is the ultimate testament to our resilience. We can learn to hold both the sorrow of the past and the joy of the present, to allow them to coexist without needing to choose between them. This is the essence of what it means to have a regulated and integrated nervous system—the ability to hold multiple, even contradictory, feeling states at once. It is a move away from the black-and-white thinking of trauma and into the rich, nuanced, and complex reality of a life fully lived. In this, we find that the experience of joy can become a resource for the parts of us that are still healing, a beacon of hope that shows them what is possible.

The research is clear on this, and it contradicts almost everything popular culture teaches.

This is not about forcing happiness or plastering over pain with positive affirmations. It is about expanding our capacity to be with the full spectrum of human experience. It is about recognizing that our joy does not diminish our sorrow, but rather, it makes the sorrow more bearable. It provides the necessary resource and resilience to continue to do the deep work of healing. For those interested in the scientific underpinnings of this, a visit to a reputable source like Psychology Today can offer further insights into the mechanics of trauma and recovery.

The Architecture of a Life That Can Hold Joy

Ultimately, the ability to sustain joy is not about a single insight or a momentary state change; it is about building a life—both internal and external—that can support it. It is about creating a container strong enough to hold the immense energy of happiness without shattering. This involves the cultivation of practices that regulate the nervous system, the development of secure relationships, and the conscious creation of a life that is aligned with our deepest values. It is about understanding that our attention is our most precious resource, and that where we place it will determine the quality of our lives.

The algorithm of your attention determines the landscape of your experience.

This means making conscious choices about the media we consume, the people we spend time with, and the way we engage with the world. It means curating our inputs to support a state of regulation rather than one of constant activation. It means learning to say no, to set boundaries, and to prioritize our own well-being, not as a selfish act, but as a necessary prerequisite for a life of meaning and connection. It is a process of de-conditioning and re-conditioning, of consciously and intentionally rewiring the pathways of the brain and nervous system. We are not passive victims of our history; we are active participants in our own becoming. And in this, there is immense power and possibility. We can learn to build a life that is not just about surviving, but about thriving, a life in which joy is not a fleeting visitor, but a welcome and abiding guest. This journey often requires a deeper understanding of our own internal world, a process we explore in our article on Internal Family Systems.

Dissolving the Old Allegiances

The final frontier in welcoming joy is often the dissolution of our allegiance to suffering. For many of us, suffering has been a primary source of identity. It has been the way we have understood ourselves, the way we have connected with others, and the lens through which we have made sense of the world. To let go of suffering can feel like letting go of who we are. It can trigger a profound identity crisis, a sense of groundlessness and disorientation. Who am I, if not the one who suffers? What will I talk about? How will I relate to the people who have known me only through my pain?

There is no version of growth that doesn't involve the dissolution of something you thought was permanent.

This is not a process to be rushed or forced. It is a gentle and gradual unfolding, a slow turning of allegiance from the familiar identity of the sufferer to the emergent identity of the one who is healing. It is a recognition that our true nature is not defined by our trauma, but by the awareness that witnesses it. The trauma is something that happened to us; it is not the totality of who we are. As we begin to dis-identify from the story of our suffering, we create space for something new to emerge. We begin to discover a self that is not defined by its wounds, but by its capacity for love, for connection, for creativity, and for joy. This is the great return, the homecoming to the wholeness that was never truly lost, only obscured. It is the discovery that the joy we sought was not something to be acquired, but something to be uncovered, a treasure buried in the field of our own being, waiting patiently for our return.

Stop reading. Close the tab. Go do the thing you have been avoiding since before you opened this article. The information will still be here when you get back. Your body will not wait forever.

Frequently Asked Questions

Why does my body tense up when something good happens?

This is a classic trauma response. Your nervous system has been wired to associate goodness or calm with subsequent danger. The tension is your body's way of bracing for an impact that it has learned to expect, even if that threat is no longer present. It's a physiological memory, not a conscious thought.

Is it possible to ever feel pure joy without suspicion?

Yes, but it is a gradual process of building new neural pathways and somatic experiences. It requires patiently teaching the body, through titrated experiences of safety and pleasure, that joy is no longer a threat. Over time, the suspicion fades as the nervous system accumulates new data that contradicts its old programming.

How do I stop sabotaging my own happiness?

The first step is to bring awareness to the pattern without judgment. Notice the impulse to create chaos when things feel calm. Instead of acting on it, get curious about the underlying fear. Often, sabotage is a misguided attempt by a part of you to return to a state that feels more familiar and therefore "safer" than the vulnerability of happiness.

The question is not how to get rid of the suspicion, but what it would mean to build a life so rooted in the present that the ghosts of the past no longer have the power to dictate the weather of your heart. What if the return of joy is not a test you have to pass, but an invitation you finally have the courage to accept?

That is your work now. Not understanding. Doing.
